title: McIlroy Makes History: Record Six-Shot Halfway Lead at Augusta
excerpt: Rory McIlroy has built a record-tying six-shot lead after 36 holes at Augusta, putting himself in pole position to become the first back-to-back Masters champion since Tiger Woods.
body:
McIlroy Takes Command at Augusta

Rory McIlroy is within touching distance of Masters immortality. The defending champion blazed a 7-under 65 on Friday to extend his lead to a record-tying six shots through 36 holes at Augusta National, with 36 holes still to play. The Northern Irishman, who won his first Green Jacket in 2025, now stands at 12-under par, leaving the rest of the field in his dust as he chases the distinction of becoming the first back-to-back Masters champion since Tiger Woods accomplished the feat in 2001 and 2002.

McIlroy Score
-12 (129)
Halfway Lead
+6 shots
Rounds This Week
67-65
Nearest Challenger
Li (-6)

The Chasing Pack Falters

China's Li Haotong sits in second place at 6-under par, eight shots adrift. Behind him, a cluster of legitimate contenders—Padraig Lowry, Sam Burns, and Cameron Young—all lurk at 8-under, but they face an insurmountable task over the weekend. Lowry registered a hole-in-one at the par-three sixth on Saturday, while Young's charge at The Players Championship just days before has him dangerous. But with McIlroy playing the golf of his life, Augusta's slopes and speeds favour the man leading, not those chasing.

Scheffler's Fade

In the tournament's biggest shock, Scottie Scheffler—the world's top-ranked player and the pre-tournament favourite at 5/1—has slipped to 7-under par after a mixed two days. Scheffler missed this week's tune-up event to await the birth of his second child, and perhaps that rustiness shows. Still, seven under remains competitive, though his chances of a second Green Jacket this side of Sunday appear bleak.

The Final Day Awaits

Augusta's Amen Corner and the treacherous closing holes have decided countless Masters. But for the first time in this tournament's 90-year history, the narrative feels written in stone. McIlroy has been asked repeatedly whether he fears throwing away this lead. His answer? The opposite. He believes momentum, confidence, and mastery of Augusta's complexities favour the leader. Sunday will test that assertion against the pressure of a major championship and the distant hopes of challengers still within reach. History beckons for McIlroy.
